title: Arguments - what you hand a function
author: Prodipta Acharjee
concept: Functions take arguments by position. A parameter can have a default value, used when the caller leaves it out.
explanation: def greet(name, greeting="hello") has a required parameter and an optional one. greet("Aria") uses the default; greet("Aria", "hey") overrides it. Defaults let one function cover the common case and the special case without you writing it twice.

exercises.question: What does this print?
hints: power(3) uses the default exp - what is it?

exercises.question:
What does this print?

def greet(name, greeting="hi"):
    return f"{greeting}, {name}"

print(greet("Aria"))
hints: Only name was given, so greeting falls back to its default.

exercises.question: When is a parameter's default value used?
options: when the caller leaves that argument out
hints: The default is a fallback, not a fixed value.
It fills in only what the caller didn't supply.
It's used when the caller leaves that argument out.
